title: One-Shot Normal Mode
subtitle: {key:Ctrl-O} runs one Normal command without leaving Insert.
summary: Ctrl-O from *insert mode* lets you fire one Normal-mode command and snap right back to insertion. The pinnacle of stay-in-flow editing.

blocks.text: {key:Ctrl-O} from *insert mode* runs *exactly one* Normal-mode command, then drops you back into Insert at the new cursor position. It's the express elevator between modes.

rows: Delete inner word, stay in Insert

blocks.title: Why It Counts as One Command
blocks.text:
The Insert-mode {key:Ctrl-O} sets an internal flag (`restart_edit`) that says: *finish exactly one Normal-mode operation, then resume Insert at the cursor*. Operator + motion still counts as one — {key:d}{key:i}{key:w} is one command. But {key:.} (dot-repeat) executed via {key:Ctrl-O} replays whatever the previous Normal command was, which can surprise you.

Note: any Insert-mode change you've already made *up to this point* is committed to the undo tree as a single edit. The next character you type starts a fresh undo unit.
{key:Ctrl-O} is what makes Vim's *insert mode* bearable. Don't fight to stay in Insert — pop out for one command and come back.
